input:
# Coastal Adaptation Cost-Effectiveness Study

## About This Report

This report responds to growing concern that municipal flood-defence budgets are being allocated without comparable evidence on which interventions actually reduce losses. It aims to compare the cost-effectiveness of seawall, managed-retreat, and hybrid green-infrastructure adaptation approaches. It is intended for municipal planners, infrastructure funders, and adaptation policy advisors. It builds on the comparative cost-benefit framework introduced by Adger et al. (2018) and follows the methodological line of recent OECD coastal-adaptation reviews. This report contributes a normalised cost-effectiveness ratio that allows direct comparison across mixed adaptation portfolios. The analysis covers five case-study cities, with physical and financial outcomes measured through 2040 only, and excludes social-equity outcomes and inland flooding scenarios.

## Methodology

The study used a comparative cost-benefit framework applied to five case-study cities.

## Findings

Hybrid green infrastructure delivered the highest normalised cost-effectiveness across all five sites.

## About the Authors

Dr. Jane Smith is a senior researcher at the Coastal Policy Institute. Her work focuses on climate adaptation and infrastructure financing. She holds a Ph.D. in environmental economics from the University of Edinburgh.

Dr. John Doe is a principal economist at the Global Risk Centre. His research interests centre on disaster risk modelling and resilience metrics. He earned his Ph.D. in applied mathematics from MIT.
target_answer: The preface satisfies all six rules (context, objectives, audience, literature, contribution, scope) and both author bios are exactly three sentences with position, affiliation, research focus, and a Ph.D. The agent should report zero issues from both the preface validator and the authors validator.
